Lung cancer – Common symptoms: persistent cough (lasting >2–3 weeks); coughing up blood or rust-colored sputum; shortness of breath; chest pain that worsens with deep breathing, coughing, or laughing; wheezing; hoarseness; recurrent or persistent chest infections (pneumonia, bronchitis); unexplained weight loss; loss of appetite; fatigue.

Colorectal (colon and rectal) cancer – Common symptoms: change in bowel habits (diarrhea, constipation, or narrower stools) lasting >2–3 weeks; blood in stool or rectal bleeding (bright red or very dark stools); feeling that the bowel does not empty completely; abdominal pain, cramping, or bloating; unexplained weight loss; fatigue; iron-deficiency anemia.

Cervical cancer – Common symptoms: abnormal vaginal bleeding (between periods, after sex, or after menopause); unusually heavy or prolonged menstrual bleeding; watery, bloody, or foul-smelling vaginal discharge; pain during sexual intercourse; pelvic or lower back pain; in advanced stages, leg swelling, difficulty urinating or defecating, or blood in urine.

Stomach (gastric) cancer – Common symptoms: persistent indigestion, heartburn, or stomach discomfort; feeling full after eating small amounts (early satiety); loss of appetite; nausea or vomiting (sometimes with blood); abdominal pain or bloating, especially after eating; unexplained weight loss; fatigue; black, tarry stools or visible blood in stool in more advanced disease.

question: Name the major battlefronts of World War II and provide a brief overview of the key events in each.
candidates:
European Western Front – 1939–1945: Germany invades Poland (1939); “Phoney War” followed by invasions of Denmark, Norway, Benelux, and France (1940); Battle of Britain (1940); Allied landings in North Africa (1942) and Italy (1943); D‑Day landings in Normandy (1944) and breakout across France; Battle of the Bulge (1944–1945); Allied advance into Germany and its surrender (1945).

Eastern Front (Germany–Soviet Union) – 1941–1945: Operation Barbarossa, Germany’s invasion of the USSR (1941); siege of Leningrad (1941–1944); Battle of Moscow (1941–1942); Battle of Stalingrad (1942–1943) turning the tide; Battle of Kursk (1943), the largest tank battle; successive Soviet offensives pushing west, including Operation Bagration (1944); fall of Berlin (1945).

Pacific Theater – 1941–1945: Japanese expansion across East Asia and the Pacific after Pearl Harbor (1941); early Japanese victories in the Philippines, Malaya, Singapore, and the Dutch East Indies; turning-point naval battles at Coral Sea (1942) and Midway (1942); island‑hopping campaigns (Guadalcanal, Tarawa, Saipan, Iwo Jima, Okinawa); U.S. submarine blockade of Japan; atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki and Soviet entry against Japan leading to Japanese surrender (1945).

China Front (Second Sino‑Japanese War) – 1937–1945: Full‑scale Japanese invasion after the Marco Polo Bridge Incident (1937); fall of major cities including Shanghai and Nanjing with widespread atrocities; prolonged stalemate and guerrilla warfare by Nationalist and Communist forces; Japanese offensives such as Operation Ichi‑Go (1944); Chinese resistance tying down large Japanese forces until Japan’s surrender.

North African Campaign – 1940–1943: Italian attack on British Egypt (1940) and British counteroffensives; arrival of Erwin Rommel and the Afrika Korps (1941); back‑and‑forth desert fighting centering on Tobruk and El Alamein; Second Battle of El Alamein (1942) halting Axis advance; Allied Operation Torch landings in Morocco and Algeria (1942); Axis retreat and surrender in Tunisia (1943).

Mediterranean and Balkan Fronts – 1940–1945: Italian invasion of Greece (1940) and German intervention in Greece and Yugoslavia (1941); Battle of Crete (1941); Allied invasion of Sicily (1943) and mainland Italy (Salerno, Anzio, 1943–1944); slow campaign up the Italian peninsula (Monte Cassino, Gothic Line); partisan warfare in Yugoslavia and Greece; eventual German withdrawal and liberation of these areas (1944–1945).

Atlantic and Naval War in Europe – 1939–1945: Battle of the Atlantic between German U‑boats and Allied convoys; early heavy Allied shipping losses; introduction of convoy systems, radar, code‑breaking (Ultra), improved air cover, and escort carriers; sinking of major German surface raiders (e.g., Bismarck, 1941); by 1943, Allied dominance securing vital supply lines between North America and Europe.

Strategic Air War (Europe and Japan) – 1940–1945: German bombing of Britain (the Blitz, 1940–1941); Allied strategic bombing of Germany targeting industry and cities (e.g., Hamburg, Dresden); combined U.S. daylight precision bombing and British night bombing; in the Pacific, U.S. B‑29 campaigns against Japan, including large‑scale incendiary raids and culminating in atomic bombings (1945).

Western European Front – 1939–45; German invasions of Poland, Denmark, Norway, the Low Countries, and France; Battle of Britain; Allied strategic bombing; 1944 D‑Day (Normandy) landings, liberation of France and the Low Countries, Battle of the Bulge, and Allied advance into western Germany. Eastern Front – 1941–45; Operation Barbarossa (German invasion of the USSR); siege of Leningrad; Battle of Moscow; turning points at Stalingrad and Kursk; massive Soviet offensives pushing German forces back, culminating in the fall of Berlin and German surrender. Mediterranean and North African Front – 1940–45; Italian attacks on Egypt and Greece; German Afrika Korps campaigns; key battles at Tobruk, El Alamein, and Tunisia; Allied invasions of Sicily and mainland Italy, Italian armistice, and slow Allied advance up the Italian peninsula. Pacific Theater – 1941–45; Japanese expansion across East and Southeast Asia and the Pacific; attacks on Pearl Harbor and other Allied bases; turning-point naval battles at Coral Sea and Midway; island‑hopping campaigns (Guadalcanal, Tarawa, Saipan, Leyte Gulf, Iwo Jima, Okinawa); strategic bombing of Japan and atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki leading to Japanese surrender. China–Burma–India (CBI) Theater – 1937–45 (Second Sino‑Japanese War integrated into WWII); large‑scale Japanese invasion and occupation of eastern China; protracted Chinese resistance; defense and reopening of supply routes (Burma Road, Hump airlift, Ledo/Stilwell Road); major battles such as Wuhan, Changsha, Imphal–Kohima, and Allied offensives in Burma. Battle of the Atlantic – 1939–45; prolonged naval and air campaign over Atlantic sea lanes; German U‑boat and surface‑raider attacks on Allied shipping; Allied convoy system, code‑breaking (Enigma), improved radar and air cover; eventual Allied control of sea routes enabling sustained supply to Britain and Europe.

Eastern Front (Germany–Soviet Union) – Opened with Operation Barbarossa (German invasion of the USSR) in 1941; key events include the sieges of Leningrad and Moscow, the Battle of Stalingrad (1942–1943) halting German advance, the Battle of Kursk (1943) as the largest tank battle, and the successive Soviet offensives (1943–1945) pushing west to capture Berlin in 1945.

Western Front (Western Europe) – Early phase saw the German invasion of Poland (1939) followed by the invasions of Denmark, Norway, the Low Countries, and France in 1940; after the fall of France, Britain resisted in the Battle of Britain (1940) and mounted air and commando raids; the front reopened with the D-Day landings in Normandy (June 1944), followed by the liberation of France, Operation Market Garden, and the German Ardennes counteroffensive (Battle of the Bulge, 1944–1945), ending with Allied advances into western Germany.

Mediterranean and North African Front – Began with Italian attacks on Egypt and Greece (1940); Germany intervened with the Afrika Korps, leading to see-saw battles such as Tobruk and Gazala; the Allies won decisive victories at El Alamein (1942) and with Operation Torch landings in North Africa, culminating in Axis surrender in Tunisia (1943); the Allies then invaded Sicily (1943), overthrew Mussolini, and fought up the Italian peninsula through battles like Monte Cassino and the Anzio landings until 1945.

Pacific Theater (island and naval war against Japan) – Started with Japanese expansion across East and Southeast Asia and the Pacific, including the attack on Pearl Harbor (1941), the fall of the Philippines, Malaya, Singapore, and the Dutch East Indies; naval turning points included the Battles of Coral Sea and Midway (1942); the Allies then advanced via “island hopping” through Guadalcanal, the Solomons, the Gilberts, Marshalls, Marianas, Philippines (Leyte Gulf), Iwo Jima, and Okinawa; ended with the atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki and Japan’s surrender in 1945.

China Front (Second Sino-Japanese War) – Began in full scale with the Marco Polo Bridge Incident (1937); Japan captured major Chinese cities such as Shanghai, Nanjing (site of the Nanjing Massacre), and Wuhan, but failed to force Chinese surrender; prolonged stalemate, guerrilla warfare, and Chinese resistance continued, aided increasingly by Allied support; major campaigns included the Japanese Ichigo Offensive (1944) and Chinese counteroffensives until Japan’s 1945 surrender.

Southeast Asian Front (Burma, Malaya, Indochina, etc.) – Japan rapidly conquered British Malaya and Singapore, Burma, the Philippines, and French Indochina (1941–1942), threatening India and cutting China’s overland supply route; key events include the Burma Campaigns, with Japanese advances on India (Imphal–Kohima, 1944) and subsequent Allied offensives recapturing Burma (1944–1945), plus resistance movements across occupied territories.

Battle of the Atlantic (naval and air campaign) – Continuous campaign (1939–1945) for control of Atlantic sea routes; German U-boats and surface raiders sought to cut Britain off from supplies, while the Allies used convoys, escorts, codebreaking (e.g., Enigma), radar, and long-range air patrols; key phases include the U-boat “Happy Time” periods, the crisis of 1942–early 1943, and the Allied technological and tactical gains that broke the U-boat threat by mid-1943.

Strategic Air War (Europe and Japan) – Large-scale bombing campaigns by the Luftwaffe against Britain (Blitz, 1940–1941) and later Allied strategic bombing of Germany targeting industry, cities, and transportation (e.g., raids on Hamburg, Dresden, the Ruhr); in the Pacific, U.S. air forces conducted a strategic bombing campaign against Japan, including the firebombing of Tokyo and other cities in 1944–1945, culminating in the atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki.

Western Front (Europe) – 1939 German invasions of Poland, Low Countries, and France; 1940 Battle of Britain; 1942–44 Allied bombing offensive; 1944 D‑Day landings in Normandy and liberation of France; 1944–45 Battle of the Bulge and Allied advance into Germany. Eastern Front – 1941 German invasion of the Soviet Union (Operation Barbarossa); 1941–42 sieges of Leningrad and Moscow; 1942–43 Battle of Stalingrad; 1943 Battle of Kursk; 1943–45 Soviet offensives pushing west, culminating in the 1945 Battle of Berlin. Mediterranean and North Africa – 1940–41 fighting in North Africa between Axis and British forces; 1941–42 Rommel’s advances and British counteroffensives; 1942 Second Battle of El Alamein turning the tide; 1943 Allied invasion of Sicily and Italy, Italian surrender, and prolonged fighting up the Italian peninsula. Pacific Theater – 1941 Japanese expansion across Southeast Asia and the Pacific after Pearl Harbor; 1942 turning-point battles at Coral Sea and Midway; 1942–44 island campaigns in Guadalcanal, New Guinea, Solomons, Gilberts, Marianas, Philippines; 1945 Iwo Jima, Okinawa, and atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki leading to Japan’s surrender. China–Burma–India Theater – 1937–45 prolonged Sino‑Japanese War in China; Japanese occupation of large Chinese areas; 1942 Japanese advances into Burma, cutting the Burma Road; Allied and Chinese counteroffensives, including the Stilwell Road and Chindit operations; 1944–45 recapture of Burma and continued Chinese resistance. Atlantic Theater (Battle of the Atlantic) – 1939–45 German U‑boat and surface raider campaign against Allied shipping; early heavy Allied losses; gradual Allied advantage through convoys, code‑breaking, air cover, and improved anti‑submarine tactics, securing supply lines from North America to Europe.

Western Front (Europe) – 1939–1940 German invasions of Poland, Denmark, Norway, the Low Countries, and France; 1940 Battle of Britain in the air; 1944–1945 Allied return with D-Day (Normandy landings), liberation of France and the Low Countries, crossing of the Rhine, and fall of western Germany; Eastern Front (Europe) – 1941 German invasion of the Soviet Union (Operation Barbarossa); 1941–1942 sieges of Leningrad and Moscow; 1942–1943 Battle of Stalingrad and Soviet counteroffensives; 1943 Battle of Kursk; 1943–1945 Soviet advance west, recapture of Eastern Europe, and fall of Berlin; Pacific Theater – 1941 Japanese attacks across the Pacific including Pearl Harbor; rapid conquests in Southeast Asia and the Pacific; 1942 turning-point naval battles of Coral Sea and Midway; 1942–1943 Guadalcanal campaign; 1943–1945 Allied “island hopping” (Gilberts, Marianas, Philippines, Iwo Jima, Okinawa) and heavy bombing of Japan, ending with atomic bombings and Japanese surrender; Mediterranean and North African Theater – 1940–1942 see-saw desert war in North Africa between Axis and British/Commonwealth (including Tobruk and El Alamein); 1943 Axis defeat in Tunisia; 1943 Allied invasion of Sicily and mainland Italy, fall of Mussolini, and prolonged fighting up the Italian peninsula; Asian Mainland / China–Burma–India – 1937–1945 Second Sino-Japanese War with large-scale Japanese offensives, Chinese resistance, and major battles like Shanghai and Wuhan; Japanese occupation of much of China; Allied campaigns in Burma (e.g., Imphal–Kohima), support to Chinese forces over “the Hump,” and reopening of land routes into China; Battle of the Atlantic – 1939–1945 German U-boat and surface raider campaign against Allied shipping; Allied convoy system, codebreaking (e.g., Enigma), and improved air/naval tactics gradually reduce U-boat threat, securing vital supply lines between North America and Europe; Strategic Air War (Europe) – 1940–1941 German Blitz against British cities; 1942–1945 Allied strategic bombing of Germany targeting industry, infrastructure, and cities (e.g., raids on Hamburg, Dresden), combined with air superiority campaigns that weaken the Luftwaffe and support ground offensives

European Theater (Western Front) – 1939–1945; Germany invades Poland (1939), then Denmark, Norway, the Low Countries, and France (1940); Battle of Britain (1940); Allied strategic bombing over Germany; invasion of North Africa (1942); invasion of Italy (1943); D‑Day and the Normandy landings (1944); liberation of Western Europe; Allied advance into Germany and German surrender (1945).

European Theater (Eastern Front) – 1941–1945; Germany invades the Soviet Union in Operation Barbarossa (1941); siege of Leningrad; Battle of Moscow; turning points at Stalingrad (1942–1943) and Kursk (1943); massive Soviet offensives push Germans west; Red Army captures Berlin, leading to Nazi Germany’s collapse (1945).

Mediterranean & North African Theater – 1940–1943; Italian attacks on British positions in North Africa; German Afrika Korps under Erwin Rommel; key battles at Tobruk, Gazala, and El Alamein (1942); Operation Torch (Allied landings in North Africa, 1942); Axis forces defeated in Tunisia (1943), enabling Allied invasions of Sicily and mainland Italy.

Italian Campaign – 1943–1945; Allied invasion of Sicily (Operation Husky, 1943) and then southern Italy; Italy surrenders and switches sides (1943) while Germans occupy much of the country; prolonged fighting along defensive lines (Gustav, Gothic); major battles at Salerno, Anzio, and Monte Cassino; eventual Allied advance to northern Italy and German surrender there (1945).

Pacific Theater – 1941–1945; Japanese expansion across East and Southeast Asia and the Pacific after attacking Pearl Harbor (1941); early Japanese victories in the Philippines, Malaya, Singapore, the Dutch East Indies; turning-point naval and air battles at Coral Sea and Midway (1942); island-hopping campaigns (Guadalcanal, Tarawa, Saipan, Iwo Jima, Okinawa); strategic bombing of Japan; atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki and Japanese surrender (1945).

China–Burma–India (CBI) Theater – 1937–1945 (with broader Sino‑Japanese War); prolonged Japanese invasion and occupation of large areas of China; major battles such as Shanghai, Wuhan, and Changsha; Allied efforts to keep China supplied via the Burma Road and “The Hump” airlift; campaigns in Burma involving British, Indian, Chinese, American, and local forces (including Chindits and Merrill’s Marauders); eventual Allied offensives recapturing Burma and tying down substantial Japanese forces.

Southeast Asian Theater – 1941–1945; rapid Japanese offensives seizing Malaya, Singapore, Burma, the Philippines, the Dutch East Indies, and other territories; harsh occupation regimes and resistance movements; later Allied counteroffensives (e.g., reconquest of the Philippines, Burma campaigns) weakening Japanese control and contributing to Japan’s defeat.

Atlantic & Arctic Naval War – 1939–1945; Battle of the Atlantic between German U‑boats/surface raiders and Allied convoys; struggle over control of sea lanes supplying Britain and later the Soviet Union; development of convoy tactics, sonar, radar, and code‑breaking (e.g., Enigma) gradually turns tide; Arctic convoys to the USSR face German naval and air attacks; German surface fleet neutralized and U‑boat threat largely defeated by 1943–1944.

Western Front (Europe) – 1939 German invasion of Poland; 1940 Blitzkrieg in Denmark, Norway, Low Countries, and fall of France; Battle of Britain; 1944–1945 Allied liberation of Western Europe after D‑Day (Normandy) and push into Germany. Eastern Front (Europe) – 1941 German invasion of the Soviet Union (Operation Barbarossa); sieges of Leningrad and Sevastopol; Battle of Moscow; turning points at Stalingrad and Kursk; Soviet offensives from 1943–1945 driving to Berlin. Mediterranean and North Africa – 1940–1943 fighting in Libya and Egypt between Axis and British/Commonwealth forces; key battles at Tobruk, El Alamein, and Kasserine Pass; Allied landings in North Africa (Operation Torch) and later invasions of Sicily and mainland Italy, including the Anzio landings and Monte Cassino. Pacific Theater (Central and South Pacific) – 1941 Japanese expansion after Pearl Harbor; early Japanese victories in the Philippines, Malaya, and Dutch East Indies; naval turning points at Coral Sea and Midway; U.S. “island‑hopping” through Guadalcanal, Tarawa, Saipan, Leyte Gulf, Iwo Jima, and Okinawa; 1945 atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki and Japan’s surrender. China–Burma–India (CBI) Front – 1937 onward Sino‑Japanese War with major battles such as Shanghai and Wuhan; Japanese occupation of large parts of China; Allied and Chinese resistance in Burma, including the Burma Road, Chindit operations, and Stilwell’s campaigns; gradual reopening of land and air supply routes to China. Atlantic and Arctic Naval War – 1939–1945 Battle of the Atlantic between German U‑boats and Allied convoys; use of convoys, radar, and codebreaking (Enigma) to reduce shipping losses; Arctic convoys supplying the Soviet Union; eventual Allied control of sea lanes enabling troop and supply movement. Strategic Bombing and Air War in Europe – sustained German bombing of Britain (the Blitz); Allied strategic bombing of Germany targeting industry, cities, and oil (e.g., Hamburg, Dresden, Ruhr); air superiority campaigns supporting ground offensives and weakening Axis war production.
